extra_findings.title: Reentrancy via external call in stake allows manipulation of initialLock and bypass of private-mode gating
extra_findings.description: The stake function performs an external call to agentNft (registry.addValidator) before completing its state updates (minting and changing totalSupply). Additionally, it conditionally sets initialLock based on totalSupply() == 0 before making the external call. Because stake has no reentrancy guard, a malicious or compromised agentNft contract can reenter stake during this external call while totalSupply is still 0. This enables: 1) Overwriting initialLock: The reentrant call executes its own initialLock = amount assignment after the outer call has already set initialLock but before any mint occurs. After the reentrant call finishes and mints, the outer call will not reassign initialLock, leaving it set to the reentrant call’s amount. This allows an attacker to set initialLock arbitrarily (either very small or very large). Impact: - Set very large initialLock to DoS the founder’s withdrawals until matureAt (founder cannot reduce their balance below the manipulated threshold). - Set very small initialLock to weaken intended lockup constraints, potentially enabling a founder to withdraw more tokens before maturity. 2) Bypassing private mode gate: The function gate require(canStake || totalSupply() == 0) is checked before the external call and minting. Multiple nested reentrant stake invocations can pass this check while totalSupply remains 0 at their respective entry points, allowing multiple stakes in what is intended to be a "first staker only" window when canStake == false. Locations: - stake(uint256 amount, address receiver, address delegatee): external call to registry.addValidator(...) occurs before transferFrom/mint/delegate. - initialLock is assigned before the external call under the condition totalSupply() == 0. Why it's a security issue: External calls before critical state updates allow reentrancy that breaks invariants and intended access control, enabling lock manipulation (DoS or premature unlocking) and defeating the private staking restriction at initialization. Potential impact: - Denial of service on founder withdrawals by inflating initialLock. - Protocol policy violation and potential investor harm by deflating initialLock. - Unauthorized multiple stakes during the initial private period. Recommended fix: Move state changes (including minting and any variables dependent on totalSupply) before any external calls, or add a reentrancy guard to stake. Additionally, compute/lock the initialLock after supply-changing operations or gate reentrant entries by using a dedicated initialization phase flag.

extra_findings.title: Unsafe ERC20 operations without return-value checks
extra_findings.description: The contract performs ERC20 operations via IERC20 without checking the boolean return values and without using SafeERC20. Specifically, migrateAgent calls IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount) and later IERC20(lp).approve(veToken, type(uint256).max). Many ERC20 tokens return false instead of reverting on failure. Ignoring these return values can cause the function to proceed under the false assumption that tokens were transferred/approved, leading to misconfigured liquidity, failed subsequent calls, or inconsistent state that may lock the migration process. Impact includes denial of service for migrations or unexpected behavior if approvals/transfers silently fail.

extra_findings.title: Unvalidated external calls to old DAO can cause migration DoS
extra_findings.description: migrateAgent reads virtualInfo.dao from the NFT and makes multiple external calls to it: oldDAO.name(), oldDAO.votingPeriod(), and oldDAO.proposalThreshold(). If the stored DAO address is a malicious or faulty contract that reverts, consumes excessive gas, or otherwise misbehaves, migration for that agent id becomes impossible (DoS). While the noReentrant guard prevents reentrancy, it does not mitigate revert-based DoS. If the virtualInfo.dao can be influenced to an arbitrary address (e.g., by prior misconfiguration or compromise), this blocks migration. Impact: denial of service for the affected agent’s migration.

extra_findings.title: Pausable is not enforced on critical operation
extra_findings.description: The contract inherits Pausable and exposes pause/unpause, but the critical migrateAgent function is not protected by whenNotPaused. In an emergency, the owner cannot pause migrations to prevent further state changes and external calls (token/LP creation, approvals, staking). This reduces the ability to mitigate active incidents. Impact: inability to halt migrations during an exploit, potentially compounding damage.

extra_findings.title: Global balance usage for custom token allows cross-application fund theft
extra_findings.description: The contract does not track per-application balances for custom tokens. In withdraw(), when handling custom-token applications, it transfers the entire IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) to the proposer, regardless of how much that particular application contributed. Similarly, in _executeApplication() for custom tokens, the router liquidity is added using IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this)) (all tokens held by the factory), not the amount associated with that application. If the factory holds the same token for multiple applications (possible via the reentrancy issue in initFromToken) or receives extra tokens accidentally, a single proposer can withdraw or consume all such tokens. This can lead to theft of tokens associated with other applications or unrelated deposits.

extra_findings.title: Fee-on-transfer/deflationary assetToken can cause DoS or misaccounting
extra_findings.description: proposeAgent() and initFromToken() set application.withdrawableAmount to applicationThreshold, assuming the contract receives exactly that amount of assetToken. If the assetToken is fee-on-transfer/deflationary, the contract will receive less than applicationThreshold. Later, withdraw() tries to return application.withdrawableAmount (applicationThreshold), which may exceed the actual balance and revert, causing a denial of service for the proposer’s withdrawal. Conversely, if the contract incidentally holds extra assetToken from other sources, the withdrawal could over-refund compared to what the proposer actually transferred. The code should compute the actual received amount via balance-before/balance-after and store that as withdrawableAmount.

extra_findings.title: Backdoored registry: hardcoded account address returned for all inputs
extra_findings.description: Both createAccount and account unconditionally return the same hardcoded address (0x9965507D1a55bcC2695C58ba16FB37d819B0A4dc), ignoring all parameters (implementation, chainId, tokenContract, tokenId, salt). This means any system relying on this registry will resolve every token-bound account to a single, attacker-controlled EOA, enabling redirection of funds and authority. Where: createAccount(...) and account(...). Why it's a security issue: ERC-6551 registries are expected to deterministically compute a unique account per (implementation, chainId, tokenContract, tokenId, salt). Returning a fixed address breaks this invariant and allows impersonation and fund redirection. Potential impact: - Loss of funds: assets sent to the supposed token account are redirected to the hardcoded EOA. - Unauthorized access/control: any authorization checks based on the resolved account address become meaningless. - Protocol manipulation: integrations that assume unique per-token accounts can be subverted.

extra_findings.title: createAccount does not deploy or initialize an account and does not revert on failure
extra_findings.description: The createAccount function returns a hardcoded address without performing any deployment (no CREATE2, no initialization) and without verifying or signaling success. Where: createAccount(...). Why it's a security issue: Callers typically expect createAccount to deploy a new account or return an existing one; here it silently returns an arbitrary EOA. This can cause downstream logic to treat a non-deployed account as valid, leading to calls failing (DoS) or assets being sent to an unintended EOA. Potential impact: - Denial of service: subsequent calls to assumed account functions will fail because the address is not a contract. - Loss of funds: funds transferred to the returned address may be irretrievable if controlled by an attacker.

extra_findings.title: External bonus distribution call can revert and block swaps (DoS vector)
extra_findings.description: During _swapForAsset(), after a successful swap and transferring creatorFee to the creator, the contract calls tbaBonus.distributeBonus(agentId, creator, creatorFee) without a try/catch. If tbaBonus is misconfigured, malicious, or reverts due to internal logic, the entire transaction reverts, undoing the swap and transfers and preventing tax processing for that agent. An ADMIN can set tbaBonus to a faulty address via updateTbaBonus; while trusted, a mistake or later compromise can cause a persistent denial of service until corrected.

extra_findings.title: Fee distribution invariant not enforced on initialization may cause unintended fund allocation
extra_findings.description: The contract enforces (feeRate + creatorFeeRate) == DENOM in updateSwapParams() but does not enforce this invariant in initialize(). initialize() sets feeRate = 100 and creatorFeeRate = 3000, which do not sum to DENOM (10000). The actual distribution uses creatorFee = assetReceived - feeAmount where feeAmount = assetReceived * feeRate / DENOM. This results in creator receiving 99% and treasury 1%, regardless of creatorFeeRate during initialization, contradicting the emitted SwapParamsUpdated2 event and potentially misallocating funds until ADMIN calls updateSwapParams() to correct it.

extra_findings.title: Treasury and creator addresses can be set to zero, leading to irreversible fund loss
extra_findings.description: updateTreasury() has no zero-address check, and updateCreator() allows setting creator to any address including address(0). Subsequent distributions (creatorFee and feeAmount) will be transferred to the zero address and irreversibly burned. While these functions are controlled (ADMIN for treasury; creator or ADMIN for creator), this creates a footgun that can lead to loss of funds due to misconfiguration or a compromised creator account intentionally griefing future payouts.

extra_findings.title: Swap threshold can be set to 0 causing repeated failing swap attempts on every transfer
extra_findings.description: setSwapThresholdBasisPoints permits setting the threshold to 0. This leads to swapThresholdInTokens being 0, making _eligibleForSwap() always true (except when _autoSwapInProgress or router/pool conditions fail), even when the contract tax balance is 0. _swapTax() will then attempt a Uniswap swap of 0 tokens, which typically reverts and is caught; however, _autoSwap() will do this on each eligible transfer. Location: setSwapThresholdBasisPoints(), _eligibleForSwap(), _autoSwap(), _swapTax(). Why it's a security issue: While caught, repeated external calls and reverts on every transfer substantially increase gas costs and can be used as an admin-triggered denial-of-service vector by making transfers prohibitively expensive. Potential impact: Denial of service via gas griefing on transfers.

extra_findings.title: Initial supply minted to deployer (msg.sender) instead of designated owner
extra_findings.description: In the constructor, the initial token supply is minted to msg.sender rather than the provided initialOwner. Specifically: ERC20._mint(msg.sender, _initialSupply); This occurs in the constructor of VirtualToken (around the minting line). If the contract is deployed via a factory, relayer, or any account different from the intended owner, the initial supply will be assigned to the deployer account, not the designated owner. This can lead to misdirected funds, potential loss of the initial supply, or reliance on the deployer to transfer the tokens to the intended owner. Impact: loss or lockup of initial tokens, unexpected centralization/trust on deployer, and potential theft if the deployer is untrusted.

extra_findings.title: Unrestricted fxManager setter enables arbitrary minting/burning and DoS
extra_findings.description:
The setFxManager function is publicly callable and lacks any access control. Any address can set _fxManager to themselves, after which they can call mint and burn to arbitrarily mint tokens to any address or burn tokens from any user without allowance. Additionally, an attacker could set _fxManager to the zero address to block legitimate mint/burn operations (Denial of Service). This occurs in setFxManager(), and is exploitable via mint() and burn() which only check msg.sender == _fxManager.
- Where: setFxManager() (public, no access control), mint() and burn() (rely on _fxManager), throughout the contract.
- Why it's a security issue: It breaks the trust boundary expected for token supply control and user balances. The contract relies on _fxManager for privileged actions, but anyone can assign themselves that role.
- Potential impact: Unlimited token inflation (loss of value), theft via forced burns from any holder (loss of funds), and protocol disruption by setting _fxManager to an unusable address (DoS).

extra_findings.title: Global token ID collisions across different DAOs due to using proposalId as tokenId
extra_findings.description: The mint function mints NFTs with tokenId equal to the provided proposalId without namespacing by virtualId/DAO. Location: mint(...), _mint(to, proposalId). Why it's a security issue: Proposal IDs are often scoped within a specific governor/DAO; two different virtual personas/DAOs could have identical proposalId values. Since tokenId is global in the NFT contract, the first proposer to mint a given proposalId will permanently prevent minting for the same numeric ID for any other DAO, causing denial of service or griefing. Potential impact: DoS on minting legitimate contributions in other DAOs when their proposalId collides; corruption of parent/child relationships referencing the wrong proposal across DAOs; inability to represent proposals from multiple DAOs concurrently.

extra_findings.title: Minting to arbitrary contract without onERC721Received can lock NFTs
extra_findings.description: The mint function uses the internal _mint (not _safeMint) to mint to info.tba. If info.tba is a contract that does not implement ERC721Receiver, the NFT can become permanently locked, as no receiver check is enforced on mint. This can cause loss of access/transferability of the asset. While this may be intentional for specific TBA designs, if info.tba is not guaranteed to be an ERC721-aware address, this is an asset-lock risk. The issue occurs in mint(uint256 virtualId, bytes32 descHash), where _mint(info.tba, proposalId) is called.

extra_findings.title: Stale and misleading kLast value (never updated on swaps)
extra_findings.description:
The pool invariant k is set only during mint() and is never updated in swap(). Thus kLast() returns a stale value that no longer reflects current reserves after any swap. If integrators or protocol logic rely on kLast as a consistency check, oracle input, or fee accounting reference, they can be misled.
- Where: mint() sets _pool.k; swap() preserves _pool.k without recomputation.
- Why it's a security issue: Stale invariants can cause dependent contracts to make unsafe assumptions (e.g., assuming AMM invariants hold) and misprice assets or miscalculate fees.
- Potential impact: Protocol manipulation and data corruption in consumers that trust kLast; incorrect fee accounting; potential economic loss for protocols using kLast as an input.

extra_findings.title: Unrestricted mint allows anyone to create unlimited tokens
extra_findings.description: The mint function is declared public without any access control and calls _mint(to, amount) directly. This occurs in the mint(address to, uint256 amount) function (around line 11). Because there is no onlyOwner or other authorization check, any caller can mint an arbitrary amount of tokens to any address. This is a severe access control flaw that enables unlimited inflation of the token supply. Potential impacts include total loss of token value, draining of liquidity pools by minting and swapping, manipulation of protocols that accept the token as collateral or for governance, and general loss of trust in the token.

extra_findings.title: Unchecked ERC20 transfer return values can silently fail and permanently mark NFT as minted
extra_findings.description:
The mint function performs token transfers via IAgentToken(tokenAddress).transfer(...) without checking the return value or using SafeERC20. If the token implements ERC20 semantics where transfer returns false on failure (without reverting), the function will proceed as if successful. Because _mintedNfts[nftId] is set to true before these transfers and the function does not revert, the NFT becomes permanently marked as minted even though no tokens were actually transferred to recipients. This results in loss of expected payouts for model/dataset owners and the IP vault, and there is no way to retry since the minted flag prevents subsequent calls.

Where: mint() function; the three transfer calls to IAgentToken(tokenAddress).transfer (model owner, dataset owner, IP vault).

Why it's a security issue: Silent transfer failures lead to incorrect state (mint recorded) and missing payouts, effectively denying funds to rightful recipients. Funds remain stranded in the contract and can be later withdrawn by the owner via saveToken, causing misallocation and potential disputes.

Potential impact: - Loss/denial of funds to model/dataset owners and IP vault
- Irreversible mint state preventing remediation without an upgrade
- Protocol integrity compromised if the underlying token returns false or is non-standard

extra_findings.title: IP share override is never applied in mint calculations
extra_findings.description:
The contract provides setIPShareOverride(uint256 virtualId, uint256 _ipShare) gated by onlyAgentDAO, storing overrides in ipShareOverrides[virtualId]. However, mint() always uses the global ipShare to compute ipAmount and never reads ipShareOverrides. This makes the onlyAgentDAO control ineffective and can lead to funds being distributed contrary to DAO-set policy, potentially overpaying or underpaying the IP vault.

Where: setIPShareOverride() sets ipShareOverrides, but mint() computes ipAmount using only the global ipShare and ignores overrides.

Why it's a security issue: Authorization intent is undermined; DAOs believe they can override IP share for their virtual agents, but it has no effect. This can cause protocol manipulation in distribution mechanics and misallocation of funds, which may constitute a financial and governance issue for participants relying on DAO-set parameters.

Potential impact: - Misallocation of funds to the IP vault or away from it
- Governance/authorization bypass of intended DAO control
- Requires upgrade to correct; affected mints cannot be rectified post-fact

extra_findings.title: Pair mapping can be overwritten to point to a new pair (duplicate pairs allowed)
extra_findings.description: The _createPair function does not check whether a pair for (tokenA, tokenB) already exists before creating and registering a new FPair. It unconditionally sets _pair[tokenA][tokenB] and _pair[tokenB][tokenA] to the newly created pair address and pushes the new pair to the pairs array. This allows a holder of CREATOR_ROLE to create multiple pairs for the same token pair and overwrite the canonical mapping returned by getPair. Location: _createPair(address tokenA, address tokenB). Security impact: Integrators and routers relying on getPair will be redirected to the latest pair, enabling protocol manipulation, user confusion, and potential loss from routing to an empty/liquidity-sparse or maliciously influenced pair, while the original pair (still in pairs array) may retain liquidity and become orphaned. Standard DEX factories enforce a "pair exists" check to maintain a single canonical pair per token pair.

extra_findings.title: Factory allows creation of pairs with identical tokens (tokenA == tokenB)
extra_findings.description: There is no check preventing tokenA and tokenB from being the same address in _createPair. Many AMM pair implementations assume distinct tokens and may misbehave or trap funds if instantiated with identical token addresses. Without seeing FPair.sol, this can lead to undefined behavior, revert loops, or value corruption. If FPair does not guard against this, a CREATOR_ROLE could create such a pair and cause denial of service for systems integrating with all pairs or lead to stuck funds. Location: _createPair(address tokenA, address tokenB) lacks a require(tokenA != tokenB) check.

extra_findings.title: Manipulable LP-based weighting of reward distribution across virtual IDs
extra_findings.description: distributeRewards() allocates the per-virtual share of the reward based on getLPValue(virtualId), which returns IERC20(rewardToken).balanceOf(lp), where lp is IAgentNft(agentNft).virtualLP(virtualId).pool. This raw token balance can be manipulated by anyone depositing or withdrawing rewardToken to/from the LP pool address at the time of distribution, skewing the relative shares among virtualIds. An attacker can temporarily inflate one virtual's pool balance (e.g., via flash liquidity or direct transfers) to capture a disproportionate fraction of the reward for that virtual and its participants, reducing others' allocation. While total funds remain within the system, this enables economic manipulation of distribution fairness and protocol incentives.

extra_findings.title: Accounting mismatch and potential shortfall with fee-on-transfer or deflationary reward tokens
extra_findings.description: distributeRewards(amount) assumes the contract receives exactly amount tokens after safeTransferFrom(). If rewardToken is fee-on-transfer/deflationary, the actual received amount will be less than amount, but internal accounting credits the full amount to rewards. Subsequent withdrawals/claims can revert due to insufficient balance or lead to partial/failed distributions, causing denial of service or unexpected behavior. The contract should either forbid fee-on-transfer tokens or measure actual received amount (balanceBefore/After) and use that for accounting.

extra_findings.title: Unbounded iteration in claim and distribution loops can lead to gas-based DoS
extra_findings.description: Several functions iterate over dynamic, potentially unbounded arrays: _getClaimableStakerRewards() and _getClaimableValidatorRewards() loop from last claimed index to rewardCount(virtualId); _distributeValidatorRewards() loops over all validators; _distributeContributorRewards() loops over all services and children. In scenarios with many rewards/validators/services, transactions to claim or distribute can exceed block gas limits, effectively freezing funds for some users or preventing distribution. Consider pagination, caps, or batching with per-claim limits.

extra_findings.title: Division-by-zero in reward calculations can permanently DoS claims
extra_findings.description: The claimable reward calculations divide by agentReward.totalStaked and agentReward.totalProposals without ensuring these values are non-zero. In _distributeAgentReward(), totalStaked is taken from nft.totalStaked(virtualId) and totalProposals from DAO.proposalCount() at distribution time, with no validation that they are > 0. If a reward is created when no one is staked (totalStaked == 0) or when there are no proposals yet (totalProposals == 0), subsequent calls to getClaimableStakerRewards() and getClaimableValidatorRewards() will revert due to division by zero. This causes denial of service for view functions and for claimStakerRewards()/claimValidatorRewards() and their 'claimAll' variants, since they rely on these calculations. Impact: Users may be unable to claim any rewards for the affected virtualId (and potentially block batch claims) until protocol state changes are made that avoid iterating over the offending reward, which is not possible given the loop traverses from the last claimed index.

extra_findings.title: Uninitialized AccessControl roles cause permanent lockout
extra_findings.description: The contract inherits OpenZeppelin AccessControl but never assigns the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE or TOKEN_SAVER_ROLE to any account. There is no constructor or initializer that calls _grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, msg.sender) or grants TOKEN_SAVER_ROLE to an operator. In AccessControl, grantRole requires the caller to have the admin role of the target role (DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE by default). Since no account is initially granted DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, no one can ever grant any roles. This occurs at the contract level (TokenSaver contract definition) and affects the onlyTokenSaver modifier and saveToken(address _token, address _receiver, uint256 _amount) function because no caller will ever satisfy hasRole(TOKEN_SAVER_ROLE, _msgSender()). As a result, the saveToken function is permanently unusable, leading to a denial of service for the intended rescue functionality and potentially causing permanent loss of recoverable tokens sent to the contract.

extra_findings.title: Duplicate validator entries allow double counting and array bloat
extra_findings.description: The _addValidator function sets _validatorsMap[virtualId][validator] = true and then unconditionally pushes the validator address into the _validators[virtualId] array. There is no check to prevent adding the same validator multiple times for the same virtualId. This occurs in _addValidator(). Because totalUptimeScore() iterates over _validators[virtualId] and sums scores for each entry, duplicate entries cause the same validator to be counted multiple times, inflating total scores and validatorCount(). This is a data integrity issue that can be exploited if an inheriting contract exposes _addValidator to untrusted actors or fails to enforce uniqueness at a higher layer. Potential impact includes manipulated governance/consensus weight, incorrect reward distribution, and in extreme cases increased gas usage and risk of DoS in functions that iterate over validators.

extra_findings.title: Stale stakingTokenToVirtualId mapping on migration can misroute protocol logic
extra_findings.description: The migrateVirtual function updates the veToken for a virtualId and sets _stakingTokenToVirtualId[veToken] = virtualId, but does not clear the previous mapping entry for the old veToken. Location: migrateVirtual(uint256 virtualId, address dao, address token, address pool, address veToken). Why it's a security issue: External integrations relying on stakingTokenToVirtualId to resolve a virtualId from a staking token can receive incorrect results for the old token after migration, enabling misassociation or protocol manipulation if the stale mapping is used (e.g., crediting actions to the wrong virtual persona). Potential impact: Data corruption/misrouting in dependent protocols, possible unauthorized effects if decisions or rewards are keyed off the mapping.

extra_findings.title: Unchecked ERC20 transfer in executeApplication can trap/deprive funds
extra_findings.description:
In executeApplication, the contract uses IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount) without SafeERC20 or checking the boolean return value. Many ERC20s return false instead of reverting on failure. If transfer returns false, the function continues: it has already set application.withdrawableAmount = 0 and application.status = Executed, preventing the proposer from withdrawing. If downstream calls (e.g., addInitialLiquidity) do not revert on zero/insufficient funds, the transaction can complete successfully while the application’s deposited asset tokens remain stuck in the factory. This results in loss of funds for the proposer and an improperly initialized agent.
- Where: executeApplication() function, at the line calling IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount)
- Why: Ignoring the return value of ERC20.transfer can silently fail; state is updated before the transfer is verified
- Impact: Loss of funds (deposits stuck in AgentFactory), corrupted initialization (agent with zero or insufficient liquidity)

extra_findings.title: Mutable assetToken after deposits leads to fund mis-accounting and potential loss
extra_findings.description:
Users deposit the current assetToken in proposeAgent via safeTransferFrom, and the application stores only an amount (withdrawableAmount) but not the token address. The admin can later call setAssetToken to change the asset token used by withdraw and executeApplication. This causes withdrawals/executions to operate on a different token than what was deposited, leaving the originally deposited tokens stranded in the factory and making users receive an unintended token or nothing (if the factory has no balance of the new token). There is no per-application binding to the originally deposited asset.
- Where: proposeAgent() (deposit), withdraw() and executeApplication() (use of assetToken), setAssetToken() (admin change)
- Why: Funds are tracked as an amount without binding to a specific token; a mutable global assetToken breaks accounting consistency
- Impact: Loss of funds (original deposits stuck), protocol/accounting corruption, potential unauthorized value shift if admin swaps assetToken to a low-value token before withdrawals

extra_findings.title: Incompatibility with fee-on-transfer tokens can lock funds
extra_findings.description:
proposeAgent transfers applicationThreshold using SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom, but sets withdrawableAmount = applicationThreshold without verifying the actual amount received. With fee-on-transfer or deflationary tokens, the contract may receive less than applicationThreshold. Later, withdraw attempts to safeTransfer the full withdrawableAmount, which reverts due to insufficient balance, causing a denial of service for refunds. Similarly, executeApplication may attempt to transfer more than the contract holds, leading to a failed or inconsistent execution.
- Where: proposeAgent() (sets withdrawableAmount = applicationThreshold), withdraw(), executeApplication()
- Why: Assumes full amount received equals requested amount; does not account for transfer fees
- Impact: Denial of service on withdrawals, funds stuck in the factory, failed or corrupted agent initialization

extra_findings.title: Pause control bypass: critical operations not pausable
extra_findings.description:
The contract is PausableUpgradeable, and proposeAgent is protected by whenNotPaused, but withdraw and executeApplication are not. During an incident or emergency, the admin cannot pause withdrawals or executions, allowing continued movement of funds and state changes even when the system is paused. This undermines incident response controls and can lead to continued loss or protocol manipulation during a pause.
- Where: withdraw() and executeApplication() lack whenNotPaused
- Why: Missing pause checks on functions that move funds and create agents
- Impact: Reduced ability to stop ongoing exploits, potential continued loss of funds or protocol manipulation during a pause

extra_findings.title: Pause mechanism can be bypassed for execution paths
extra_findings.description: The contract uses PausableUpgradeable and gates proposeAgent() and initFromBondingCurve() with whenNotPaused, but executeApplication() (and by extension _executeApplication()) is not gated by pause. During an incident, the admin cannot halt application execution, which involves creating new clones, transferring funds, minting NFTs, creating TBAs, and staking LP tokens. Why it's a security issue: A pause is typically relied upon to stop critical state transitions and fund movements. Missing pause checks on execute paths allow continued operations even when the system is paused, reducing the effectiveness of emergency response. Potential impact: Inability to halt fund flows and agent creation during emergencies, potentially exacerbating losses or protocol manipulation.

extra_findings.title: Auto-execution during vote can revert and block the final vote transaction
extra_findings.description: _tryAutoExecute() is called inside _castVote() when support == 1. If forVotes equals the token total supply at the snapshot, execute(proposalId) is invoked immediately within the voting call. If the proposal's execution payload reverts, the entire vote-casting transaction reverts, meaning the 'last' vote that would have brought forVotes to total supply cannot be recorded. This creates a denial-of-service for that final voter and may repeatedly block any attempt to cast the final vote while keeping the proposal active until the voting period ends. While the proposal can still be executed later (after the voting period) if it meets success conditions, this behavior is unexpected and can censor the final vote and degrade UX.

extra_findings.title: Reentrancy risk in buy() and sell() via external router calls can corrupt token state and influence graduation
extra_findings.description:
The functions buy(uint256,address) and sell(uint256,address) perform external calls to router.buy(...) and router.sell(...) without a reentrancy guard. An untrusted or compromised router (or tokens with malicious behaviors) could reenter these functions before they finish, causing multiple inconsistent updates to tokenInfo[tokenAddress].data (price, marketCap, liquidity, volume, prevPrice, lastUpdated). In buy(), this state is also used to decide when to trigger _openTradingOnUniswap (graduation) based on computed reserves. A reentrant call could manipulate the sequence and timing of updates to influence or prematurely trigger graduation checks, or to otherwise corrupt accounting data relied upon by off-chain or subsequent on-chain logic. While _openTradingOnUniswap sets flags (trading=false, tradingOnUniswap=true) early to mitigate double-execution inside that function, the lack of a nonReentrant guard on buy/sell still allows reentrant re-invocations that can result in data corruption and protocol manipulation.

Where: 
- buy(uint256 amountIn, address tokenAddress) (around the call to router.buy and subsequent state updates)
- sell(uint256 amountIn, address tokenAddress) (around the call to router.sell and subsequent state updates)

Why it's a security issue: Reentrancy through external/router calls can cause repeated or out-of-order state updates, leading to corrupted price/liquidity/volume metrics and potentially manipulating graduation logic. This can be exploited to advance a token to graduation earlier or interfere with expected trading state, impacting protocol integrity and participants relying on these states.

Potential impact:
- Protocol manipulation: premature or blocked graduation based on corrupted state
- Data corruption: inaccurate price/volume/liquidity stored on-chain
- Potential denial-of-service of state-dependent features if values cause arithmetic edge cases or unexpected reverts

Recommendation: Add nonReentrant to buy and sell, or refactor to use the checks-effects-interactions pattern strictly, moving external calls after necessary state changes where safe, and/or restrict trusted router addresses.

extra_findings.title: Executor can pull funds from any approved address via 'to' parameter in buy/sell
extra_findings.description: Both buy and sell functions use the 'to' parameter as the payer for token transfers (via safeTransferFrom) rather than the caller. In sell, token.safeTransferFrom(to, pairAddress, amountIn) pulls the input tokens from 'to'. In buy, IERC20(assetToken).safeTransferFrom(to, pair, amount) and IERC20(assetToken).safeTransferFrom(to, feeTo, txFee) pull the asset and fee from 'to'. Because these functions are restricted to EXECUTOR_ROLE and do not require to == msg.sender, any executor can specify an arbitrary victim address 'to' and drain their approved allowances without the victim initiating the transaction. This enables loss of funds for any user who has granted allowances to the router (a common pattern for routers), contrary to user expectations that their funds are only used when they call the router themselves.

extra_findings.title: External call to taxManager can cause DoS on swaps when feeTo == taxManager
extra_findings.description: Both buy and sell call IBondingTax(taxManager).swapForAsset() if feeTo == taxManager. This is an unguarded external call; if taxManager is set to an address that reverts or is misconfigured, all buy/sell transactions (when feeTo equals taxManager) will revert after performing transfers to the pair and before completing the swap, effectively causing a denial of service for trading through the router. There is no try/catch, no rate-limiting, and no circuit breaker.

extra_findings.title: Potential division-by-zero and unexpected reverts in getAmountsOut
extra_findings.description: getAmountsOut divides by newReserveA/newReserveB computed from current reserves and amountIn. If reserves are zero and/or amountIn is zero, division by zero will occur, causing a revert. The sell function does not validate amountIn > 0 and can call getAmountsOut with amountIn == 0, leading to a revert. While this does not directly lead to fund loss, it can be used to trigger unnecessary reverts and disrupt expected flow.

extra_findings.title: Division-by-zero in contributor distribution when coreTypes is empty
extra_findings.description: In _distributeContributorRewards(), the code sets reward.coreAmount = amount / coreTypes.length without checking coreTypes.length > 0. If an agent has no coreTypes, this division reverts and causes the entire distribution transaction that includes this agent to fail. Since distributeRewardsForAgents() loops over supplied virtualIds, including such an agent will make the call revert, blocking distributions and causing a DoS on reward processing for that batch.

extra_findings.title: Division by zero when querying maturity with zero forVotes
extra_findings.description: getMaturity() divides _proposalMaturities[proposalId] by forVotes obtained from proposalVotes(proposalId). If forVotes is zero (e.g., before any supportive vote is cast), this will revert due to division by zero, causing a read-time DoS for this view function. Although it does not impact funds or control flow of governance, it can break integrators or UI components that expect a safe read.

extra_findings.title: Unrestricted minting via syncDeposit allows unlimited token inflation
extra_findings.description: The syncDeposit function is publicly callable and directly mints tokens to the caller without any authentication or message verification. Specifically, in syncDeposit(address childToken, uint256 amount) (around lines 8-13), the contract instantiates BMWTokenChild at the provided address and calls childTokenContract.mint(msg.sender, amount). There is no access control (e.g., only bridge, only fxChild) and no proof validation (e.g., Merkle proof of an L1 deposit). If BMWTokenChild restricts minting to this tunnel (as is typical), any user can call this function to mint arbitrary amounts of the child token to themselves by passing the legitimate child token address. This completely bypasses intended bridge security, leading to unlimited inflation, loss of value for legitimate holders, and protocol manipulation.

extra_findings.title: No length check between recipients and amounts arrays can cause unintended burns
extra_findings.description:
The contract does not validate that _recipients and _amounts have the same length. In assembly, the loop uses sz := _amounts.length and then reads both _amounts[i] and _recipients[i] using that index. If _amounts is longer than _recipients, calldataload for _recipients[i] beyond its length will return 0, causing transfers to the zero address (burn) for those extra entries. This results in irreversible loss of tokens.
Where: airdrop() function, loop over arrays (assembly block), around the lines reading calldataload(add(_recipients.offset, offset)) without prior length equality check.
Why it's a security issue: A caller or front-end mistake can irreversibly burn funds by providing mismatched array lengths. Malicious actors could also craft calldata to intentionally cause burns.
Potential impact: Permanent loss of tokens sent to address(0) for any indices where recipients are out-of-bounds.

extra_findings.title: Caller-supplied _total not validated against sum(amounts) leads to trapped funds
extra_findings.description:
The function pulls _total tokens from the caller via transferFrom before distributing amounts, but never verifies that _total equals the sum of _amounts. If _total exceeds the sum of amounts, the transaction will succeed and leave the surplus tokens stranded in the Airdrop contract with no way to recover them (no withdraw or recovery function). If _total is less than the sum, the loop will likely revert mid-distribution, which reverts the whole transaction, but the first case causes permanent loss.
Where: airdrop() function, assembly before the loop performs transferFrom for _total, and no post-check ensures full distribution.
Why it's a security issue: Users can unintentionally send more tokens than needed, resulting in tokens being stuck forever in the contract.
Potential impact: Permanent loss of funds for the caller equal to _total - sum(_amounts).

extra_findings.title: Owner-configurable datasetImpactWeight can cause updateImpact to revert (DoS via misconfiguration)
extra_findings.description: setDatasetImpactWeight allows the owner to set any uint16 as datasetImpactWeight, which is used as a basis points divider by 10000. If weight > 10000, then _impacts[proposalId] = rawImpact - (rawImpact * weight / 10000) will underflow and revert (Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic). This would brick updateImpact for the affected entries, resulting in denial of service for impact updates and any downstream logic relying on them. Although owner-controlled, adding a sanity check (e.g., weight <= 10000) prevents accidental DoS. The issue occurs in setDatasetImpactWeight(uint16 weight) and its usage in updateImpact().

extra_findings.title: Non-standard ERC721 ownerOf behavior returns address(0) instead of reverting
extra_findings.description: The contract overrides ownerOf to return _ownerOf(tokenId), which returns address(0) for nonexistent or burned tokens, instead of reverting as mandated by the ERC721 standard. Location: ownerOf(uint256) function. Why it's a security issue: Many integrations rely on the ERC721 standard behavior that ownerOf reverts for nonexistent tokens to validate token existence. Returning address(0) can cause external protocols or application logic to mis-handle invalid tokenIds, potentially bypassing checks (e.g., code that expects a revert might treat address(0) as a valid result or use ownerOf != address(0) incorrectly). Potential impact: Unauthorized access or logic bypass in integrated systems, incorrect accounting, or protocol manipulation due to nonstandard behavior.

extra_findings.title: ownerOf() revert can block vote casting with params
extra_findings.description: In _updateMaturity(), the contract calls IERC721(contributionNft).ownerOf(proposalId) and then checks if the returned owner is address(0) to early return. However, ERC721 ownerOf() reverts for non-existent token IDs and never returns address(0). Because _updateMaturity() is invoked during _castVote() when params.length > 0 and support == 1, any vote that includes non-empty params for a proposalId that is not a valid ERC721 token ID will revert, preventing that vote from being cast. This can be triggered by the voter themselves (griefing their own transaction) but also unintentionally breaks the intended maturity-voting flow for proposals that don't correspond 1:1 to NFT token IDs. Impact: users cannot cast a supportive vote with params on such proposals; if your design expects params on votes, this becomes a practical DoS on casting those votes.

extra_findings.title: Duplicate txhash protection can be bypassed for agentId = 0
extra_findings.description: The contract uses taxHistory[txhash].agentId > 0 to detect whether a txhash was already processed. If agentId can legitimately be 0, the check will not detect duplicates for that agent, allowing the same txhash to be reused and recorded multiple times. This occurs in handleAgentTaxes() when iterating over txhashes. This is a security issue because it undermines the intended idempotency/uniqueness of tax collection records, enabling data corruption and potential over-accounting. While actual swaps still require sufficient token balance and the whole transaction reverts on insufficient balance, the logical protection against duplicate txhashes is ineffective for agentId 0, which can lead to incorrect accounting and potential protocol manipulation if agentId 0 is valid.

extra_findings.title: Initial liquidity protection can be bypassed via alternate liquidity pool
extra_findings.description: The pre-launch transfer restriction only blocks transfers to the specific uniswapV2Pair address before fundedDate is set. Attackers can create a different liquidity pool (e.g., with the same pairToken or even another token) and add liquidity before the official initial liquidity is added, because _pretaxValidationAndLimits only checks `to_ == uniswapV2Pair`. Location: _pretaxValidationAndLimits(), condition `if (to_ == uniswapV2Pair && from_ != address(this) && fundedDate == 0) { revert ... }`. Why it's a security issue: This allows front-running the intended launch mechanics, enabling price manipulation, premature trading, and undermining any bot protection assumptions tied to the official pool. Potential impact: Protocol manipulation and potential losses for users interacting with manipulated liquidity and price prior to official launch.

extra_findings.title: Unbounded iteration over validators can cause gas-based DoS in callers
extra_findings.description: The totalUptimeScore(uint256 virtualId) function iterates over the entire _validators[virtualId] array and sums scores. If the array becomes large, calls to totalUptimeScore() can run out of gas. While totalUptimeScore() is a public view function, it can be invoked internally by state-changing functions in inheriting contracts, or its result may be required on-chain by other contracts, leading to transactions that revert due to gas exhaustion. Additionally, the duplicate-entries issue in _addValidator() can exacerbate growth. The practical impact is denial-of-service for any on-chain logic depending on totalUptimeScore(), making parts of the protocol unusable once the validator set grows sufficiently.

extra_findings.title: Unchecked division-by-zero in rpow silently returns incorrect results
extra_findings.description: The rpow function performs divisions by the scalar parameter using EVM div in assembly without checking that scalar != 0. In EVM, div(x, 0) returns 0 instead of reverting. As a result, when scalar is 0, intermediate divisions x := div(xxRound, scalar) and z := div(zxRound, scalar) will silently set x and/or z to 0, and the function will return an incorrect result (typically 0) instead of reverting. This occurs within rpow(), at the assignments that divide by scalar. This is a security issue because callers may reasonably assume division-by-zero reverts (as in Solidity), and logic that relies on rpow for interest/price accrual, discounting, or exponentiation could be forced into returning 0 by passing a zero scalar, leading to protocol manipulation, mispricing, or loss of funds depending on usage. Potential impact includes incorrect accounting or payout calculations if scalar can be set or influenced to zero by user input or configuration mistakes.

extra_findings.title: uint8 overflow in core type indexing can overwrite existing entries
extra_findings.description: The contract uses a uint8 counter (_nextCoreType) to index coreTypes. In both __CoreRegistry_init() and _addCoreType(), the counter is incremented using the ++ operator and used as the key in the coreTypes mapping. Because uint8 wraps around after 255, adding more than 256 core types will cause _nextCoreType to overflow back to 0 and begin overwriting previously stored entries. This occurs in: variable _nextCoreType (declaration) and its use in __CoreRegistry_init() and _addCoreType(). This is a security issue because it can corrupt registry data, potentially leading to misclassification, loss of referential integrity, or protocol logic errors if other components rely on stable, immutable core type IDs. Impact: data corruption and potential denial of service for systems depending on unique, non-overlapping IDs.

extra_findings.title: Owner can arbitrarily mint, burn, and reassign balances via oracleTransfer
extra_findings.description: The oracleTransfer function (oracleTransfer(address[] froms, address[] tos, uint256[] values)) is restricted to onlyOwner but allows the owner to call the internal _update function with arbitrary from/to/value triples. Because _update in OpenZeppelin ERC20 semantics treats from == address(0) as mint and to == address(0) as burn, the owner can: (1) mint unlimited tokens to any address (0x0 -> user), (2) burn tokens from any address (user -> 0x0), and (3) transfer tokens between arbitrary addresses without their consent (userA -> userB). This enables full centralized control over all balances and total supply. If the owner key is compromised or malicious, they can seize user balances, inflate supply, or manipulate governance outcomes since this token includes ERC20Votes. Potential impact includes loss of funds (token seizure), governance manipulation through arbitrary minting/transfers, and supply corruption. While this may be intended for cross-chain syncing, it is a high-risk trust and access-control issue unless additional off-chain/contractual guarantees exist. Consider restricting zero-address use, separating roles (oracle vs mint/burn), adding timelocks, multi-sig ownership, or on-chain proofs for movements.

extra_findings.title: Missing sanity checks for min/max swap thresholds can cause reverts and logic bypass
extra_findings.description: The contract allows setting arbitrary minSwapThreshold and maxSwapThreshold without validation. In swapForAsset, after ensuring amount >= minSwapThreshold, if amount > maxSwapThreshold, amount is set to maxSwapThreshold without re-checking > 0. If maxSwapThreshold is set to 0 (or less than minSwapThreshold), a valid amount can be reduced to 0, leading to a call to router.getAmountsOut(0, path), which typical routers revert on, causing swapForAsset to revert (DoS). Additionally, when maxSwapThreshold < minSwapThreshold, the min threshold control is effectively bypassed for amounts > max, enabling unintended small swaps. Occurs in updateSwapThresholds (no validation) and swapForAsset (threshold handling before calling router).

extra_findings.title: Incorrect event parameter order in BonusRateUpdated emission
extra_findings.description: The updateBonusRate function emits the BonusRateUpdated event with parameters reversed. The event is defined as BonusRateUpdated(uint16 oldBonusRate, uint16 newBonusRate), but the function emits BonusRateUpdated(bonusRate_, oldBonusRate). This occurs in updateBonusRate(). This is a data integrity/security issue because off-chain indexers, monitoring, governance, or accounting systems relying on the event to track configuration changes will record incorrect values, potentially leading to wrong decisions or automated actions (e.g., misconfigured payout logic, audits missing a malicious rate change). While funds are not directly lost by this bug, it can facilitate off-chain manipulation or misreporting.

extra_findings.title: Missing zero-address validation in initialize can brick roles or token
extra_findings.description: The initialize function does not validate defaultAdmin_ or assetToken_. If defaultAdmin_ is the zero address, administrative control is effectively lost: roles are granted to the zero address, preventing any future role management, updates to bonus rates, or allowance adjustments. If assetToken_ is the zero address or a non-contract, bonus distribution will revert when attempting transfers, causing a denial of service for payouts. This occurs in initialize(). The impact can be protocol paralysis (inability to manage roles or distribute) due to misconfiguration; while not an external exploit, it is a security footgun with high operational risk.

extra_findings.title: Initializer callable by anyone (risk of initialization front-running)
extra_findings.description: The initialize function is external and protected only by the initializer modifier. If this contract is deployed directly (not via a proxy with an initializer call in the same transaction), an attacker could front-run and call initialize first, setting themselves as the default admin and ADMIN_ROLE and selecting arbitrary token and agentNft addresses. This enables unauthorized control and could route user funds to attacker-controlled addresses if users later interact with the contract. Best practice is to ensure initialization is performed atomically (e.g., via a proxy's constructor) and/or include a constructor that calls _disableInitializers() on the implementation to prevent unintended initialization. Impact: unauthorized admin control and potential funds redirection if mis-deployed.

extra_findings.title: unsafeDiv/unsafeMod/unsafeDivUp return 0 on division-by-zero, masking errors
extra_findings.description: The helper functions unsafeMod, unsafeDiv, and unsafeDivUp intentionally use EVM mod/div semantics that return 0 when y == 0, instead of reverting like Solidity would. While documented as 'unsafe', this behavior can mask bugs or enable logic bypasses if callers assume revert-on-zero-division semantics. Specifically: unsafeMod(x, y) returns 0 for y == 0; unsafeDiv(x, y) returns 0 for y == 0; unsafeDivUp(x, y) returns 0 for y == 0. If these functions are used in critical calculations (e.g., fee, price, or share calculations) with user-controlled or dynamic denominators, an attacker could trigger y == 0 to force a 0 result and manipulate downstream logic, potentially causing loss of funds or incorrect state updates.

extra_findings.title: Division-by-zero in priceALast() and priceBLast()
extra_findings.description:
The view functions priceALast() and priceBLast() divide by _pool.reserve0 and _pool.reserve1 respectively without checking for zero. If either reserve is zero (e.g., after a swap that sets a reserve to zero or an initial mint with a zero reserve), these functions will revert. This creates a denial-of-service condition for any integrator relying on these read functions for pricing or health checks.
- Where: priceALast() and priceBLast() functions
- Why it's a security issue: External systems (or the router) may depend on these views for pricing/oracle reads. A revert can break downstream protocols, trigger liquidations, or halt operations relying on these calls.
- Potential impact: DoS on read paths, oracle failures, interruptions in upstream protocol logic that assumes these calls do not revert.

extra_findings.title: Unrestricted setters allow arbitrary manipulation of factory outputs
extra_findings.description: The functions setMockAgentToken(address) and setMockId(uint256) are externally callable by anyone and lack access control. These setters directly influence the return values of key interface functions: executeBondingCurveApplication(...) returns the attacker-controlled mockAgentToken, and proposeAgent(...) / initFromBondingCurve(...) return the attacker-controlled mockId. This enables any external account to manipulate protocol-facing outputs, potentially pointing dependent systems to a malicious token address or falsifying agent IDs. If other contracts or off-chain services rely on these interface outputs for critical flows (e.g., deploying/initializing agents or routing funds), an attacker can cause protocol manipulation, misdirection of interactions, or denial of service via invalid addresses/IDs. This occurs in setMockAgentToken and setMockId, and impacts executeBondingCurveApplication, proposeAgent, and initFromBondingCurve.

extra_findings.title: Lack of validation for implementation and config addresses can brick migrations
extra_findings.description: Administrative setters (setInitParams and setImplementations) accept addresses without validation. Setting zero addresses or non-contract addresses for tokenImplementation, veTokenImplementation, daoImplementation, _assetToken, or _uniswapRouter will cause Clones.clone or subsequent calls to revert, permanently blocking migrations until corrected. While this is an admin misconfiguration risk, it has protocol-level DoS impact if mis-set or set maliciously by a compromised owner.

extra_findings.title: Manual refund bypasses duplicate-refund protection
extra_findings.description:
manualRefund increments refunds[txhash] without any existence or duplication checks, unlike refund which attempts to prevent reusing a txhash. This allows an ADMIN_ROLE to process the same txhash multiple times.

Where: manualRefund(bytes32, address, uint256)
Why it's a security issue: It undermines the contract's duplicate-prevention logic and increases risk of accidental or abusive double refunds by privileged actors.
Potential impact: Privileged users can unintentionally or intentionally double-refund the same txhash, leading to fund loss or accounting inconsistencies. While ADMIN_ROLE is trusted, this design makes mistakes easier and defeats the apparent uniqueness invariant.

extra_findings.title: Owner/factory can arbitrarily increase tax rates (potential 100% confiscation)
extra_findings.description: The setProjectTaxRates function allows the owner or factory to set the buy and sell tax basis points to any uint16 value without enforcing decreases only or any upper bound. Despite the comment claiming taxes are 'subject to only ever decreasing', there is no such check. Location: setProjectTaxRates(), AgentToken.sol. Why it's a security issue: An admin can raise taxes up to 100% at any time, effectively confiscating all tokens from users on buys/sells by redirecting the taxed amount to the contract and then to the projectTaxRecipient via auto-swap or distributeTaxTokens. Potential impact: Loss of user funds through arbitrary taxation and redirection to the projectTaxRecipient (rug risk).

extra_findings.title: Reentrancy risk in admin withdrawals due to state update after external transfer
extra_findings.description: withdrawProtocolRewards() and withdrawValidatorPoolRewards() perform IERC20(rewardToken).safeTransfer(recipient, amount) before zeroing protocolRewards/validatorPoolRewards. If rewardToken is ERC777 or a token with callback, and recipient is a contract, its hook can reenter the function before the state is zeroed and trigger a second transfer of the same amount. Although only callable by GOV_ROLE, a malicious or compromised governor (or a contract governor with hooks) could drain more than intended or leave the contract underfunded relative to accounting. State should be updated before external calls or protected by a reentrancy guard.

extra_findings.title: Missing access control on syncWithdraw allows arbitrary token drain
extra_findings.description: The syncWithdraw function is publicly callable and transfers any specified amount of any ERC20 token held by the contract to the caller without any authorization or balance accounting. Specifically, in syncWithdraw(address rootToken, uint256 amount), the contract calls IERC20(rootToken).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) directly. There is no verification that the caller is authorized (e.g., bridge, owner) or that the amount corresponds to a validated withdrawal event or a recorded user balance. As a result, any attacker can call syncWithdraw repeatedly to drain all tokens of any rootToken stored in the contract. Impact: complete loss of funds held by this contract.

extra_findings.title: Application deposit can be withdrawn almost immediately, enabling spam/DoS on application registry
extra_findings.description: When proposing an agent (proposeAgent or initFromBondingCurve), proposalEndBlock is set to the current block number. The withdraw() function only requires block.number > application.proposalEndBlock. This allows a proposer to withdraw in the very next block, recovering the entire deposit. As a result, the applicationThreshold provides little economic deterrence against spamming many applications; attackers can create numerous applications and reclaim funds shortly after, paying only gas. Why it's a security issue: This design enables a low-cost spam vector, potentially bloating on-chain storage and event logs and causing operational or UX degradation. Potential impact: Denial of service or resource exhaustion via a large number of bogus applications with minimal economic cost.

extra_findings.title: Unrestricted initializer allows takeover of roles if not called immediately after deployment
extra_findings.description: The initialize function is external and grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to msg.sender without any access restriction beyond the initializer modifier. If this contract is deployed behind a proxy and initialize is not executed by the intended deployer immediately, any attacker can call initialize first, become the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE holder, grant themselves ADMIN_ROLE and CREATOR_ROLE, set arbitrary router and tax parameters, and fully control pair creation. This creates a complete protocol takeover risk. This issue occurs in initialize(address taxVault_, uint256 buyTax_, uint256 sellTax_). While the initializer pattern is standard, leaving it publicly callable means a single missed initialization during deployment results in loss of control.

extra_findings.title: Missing storage gap in upgradeable base contract can risk storage layout corruption on upgrade
extra_findings.description: CoreRegistry is designed for use with upgradeable proxies (inherits Initializable), but it lacks a reserved storage gap (e.g., uint256[50] private __gap;). In upgradeable contracts, especially abstract base contracts intended to be inherited, adding new state variables in future versions can shift the storage layout, corrupting existing state or colliding with child contract storage. Without a storage gap in this base, future upgrades to this contract or its inheritance tree may inadvertently overwrite _nextCoreType or coreTypes, leading to data corruption or malfunction. This issue is located at the contract-level storage layout (no __gap declared).
